caveats: a: BLS occupations cover NAICS 212 rather than 212390 or a verified outsourced subset.
a: ILO exposure measures technical potential, not current unautomated work.
a: The frozen labor ratio may blend very different mineral processes within 21239.
rho: No 212390-specific AI implementation study was found.
rho: Implementation differs sharply among underground salt, open-pit phosphate, brine, peat, and specialty-mineral operations.
e: Commodity producer and mine counts are not a substitute for the missing LMM denominator.
e: Eligible contract operators may be correctly classified outside 212390.
s5: Mine or mineral-right asset sales are not necessarily transfers of eligible operating firms.
s5: Large producer transactions do not proxy LMM owner succession.
q: Commodity prices do not reveal contractual pass-through of labor productivity.
q: Commercial terms from NAICS 213115 cannot be assumed to represent an unidentified 212390 population.
d5: Commodity volumes do not directly measure outsourced service demand.
d5: Aggregate results can conceal opposing trends across salt, phosphate, potash, barite, borates, peat, and specialty minerals.
d5: Weather materially affects road-salt demand, while energy and agriculture cycles affect other commodities.
o: The estimate separates generative-AI substitution from conventional equipment automation.
o: The screened operator population itself remains unverified.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The required service model would consist of repeat operating or toll-beneficiation contracts priced by ton, campaign, capacity, or fixed operating fee. Public classification evidence indicates that contract or fee-based mining support ordinarily belongs outside 212390, so no in-code revenue model was verified.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to outsourced extraction and beneficiation because 212390 combines highly dissimilar mineral product businesses and the fixed lens requires recurring outsourced service. Census expressly classifies contract or fee-based support for nonmetallic mining in 213115, leaving the existence of an eligible 212390 population unresolved.
lens.excluded_activities: Mineral owners selling their own output as a commodity or proprietary product, captive mines, mineral-rights holding vehicles, post-beneficiation chemical or mineral manufacturing, and contract or fee-based support activities classified by Census in NAICS 213115.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms, if any, supplying repeat outsourced mine-site development, extraction, milling, or beneficiation of nonmetallic minerals such as barite, natural borates, phosphate rock, potash, peat, rock salt, and dry-lake brines for external mineral owners or industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupations for the broader mining-except-oil-and-gas subsector, concentrated in equipment operation, extraction supervision, and truck driving, are combined with ILO broad-occupation GenAI exposure to infer a wage-weighted opportunity for hypothetical outsourced 212390 operations. This can overstate exposure where work is predominantly field-based and understate it where geology, process control, permitting, logistics, and commercial roles carry higher wages.
caveats: BLS occupations cover NAICS 212 rather than 212390 or a verified outsourced subset.
ILO exposure measures technical potential, not current unautomated work.
The frozen labor ratio may blend very different mineral processes within 21239.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation roster and task-time study from representative salt, phosphate, potash, barite, borate, and other mineral operations, including their current automation baseline, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can assist geological interpretation, fleet dispatch, maintenance analysis, process monitoring, quality and environmental reporting, scheduling, procurement, and administration, while extraction, hauling, milling, brine operation, and equipment maintenance remain physical.

d5.bridge: USGS production, consumption, end-use, and critical-mineral evidence for major 212390 commodities is translated into year-five demand for hypothetical outsourced extraction and beneficiation. It can overstate the lens if producers retain captive operations and understate it if new domestic supply-chain projects use specialist operators.
caveats: Commodity volumes do not directly measure outsourced service demand.
Aggregate results can conceal opposing trends across salt, phosphate, potash, barite, borates, peat, and specialty minerals.
Weather materially affects road-salt demand, while energy and agriculture cycles affect other commodities.
d5.change_evidence: Mineral-specific five-year mine plans, reserve depletion, customer forecasts, drilling activity, fertilizer demand, road-deicing volumes, new-project schedules, and verified outsourced operating awards would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The category spans stable salt demand, fertilizer-linked phosphate and potash, drilling-linked barite, and numerous specialty minerals. USGS reports 2025 salt production near its 2021 level, while phosphate apparent consumption was below 2024 and domestic reserves and import reliance create offsetting pressures; heterogeneity warrants a wide range around flat demand.

caveats: Commodity producer and mine counts are not a substitute for the missing LMM denominator.
Eligible contract operators may be correctly classified outside 212390.
e.change_evidence: A verified establishment-to-firm roster with EBITDA, mineral ownership, customer contracts, operating-fee revenue, captive status, and reviewed NAICS classification is required.
e.rationale: The frozen LMM firm count is explicitly missing, and Census places contract or fee-based support for nonmetallic-mineral mining in NAICS 213115. Public sources do not establish a denominator or identify any 212390 firms that nonetheless meet the recurring outsourced-service lens.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 21239: labor compensation (L02) $1,625.4M / sectoral output (T30) $6,052.1M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.2936 (margins.json[21]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Metals & Mining EBITDA/Sales 29.36%); revenue/employee $575,535 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 21239); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate separates generative-AI substitution from conventional equipment automation.
The screened operator population itself remains unverified.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on customer insourcing, autonomous operations, import displacement, recycling, mineral substitution, mine closures, tolling renewals, and new domestic capacity would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Extraction, brine handling, milling, beneficiation, safety, environmental compliance, quality control, and delivery remain physical responsibilities. AI can improve workflows, but customer insourcing, conventional autonomous equipment, recycling, imports, and mineral substitution can reduce the quantity requiring the hypothesized operator.

caveats: Commodity prices do not reveal contractual pass-through of labor productivity.
Commercial terms from NAICS 213115 cannot be assumed to represent an unidentified 212390 population.
q.change_evidence: Verified 212390 operating or toll-beneficiation agreements showing pricing basis, escalation, open-book terms, renewal cadence, cost-down sharing, and customer concentration are required.
q.rationale: No eligible in-code outsourced service population or representative contract structure was verified. Estimating retained benefit from commodity mineral pricing would measure a different business model, while fee-based mining support is expressly classified outside 212390.

caveats: No 212390-specific AI implementation study was found.
Implementation differs sharply among underground salt, open-pit phosphate, brine, peat, and specialty-mineral operations.
rho.change_evidence: Mine-level pilots reporting adoption, uptime, recovery, quality variance, safety incidents, reporting time, headcount avoidance, integration cost, and worker acceptance would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Planning, document retrieval, maintenance support, dispatch, quality reporting, and administration are implementable within five years, but remote operations, safety requirements, weak data infrastructure, environmental controls, legacy equipment, and mineral-specific process variation constrain adoption.

caveats: Mine or mineral-right asset sales are not necessarily transfers of eligible operating firms.
Large producer transactions do not proxy LMM owner succession.
s5.change_evidence: After establishing eligible firms, target owner ages, mineral-right relationships, shareholder structure, succession plans, sponsor ownership, broker mandates, and five-year control transactions would be required.
s5.rationale: Because no defensible eligible 212390 service population was identified, a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability cannot be bounded without importing unrelated ownership or transaction statistics.

principal_driver: Persistent physical demand for salt, fertilizer feedstocks, drilling minerals, and diverse specialty nonmetallic inputs.
principal_weakness: The recurring outsourced-service business model appears to be classified outside 212390, leaving no defensible eligible firm or contract population.
